1.lecture:
§1 · The drawing is the contract

A drawing is not a picture of a part; it is a contract about a part. It binds a designer's intent, a fabricator's obligation, an inspector's acceptance criteria, and — years later — a maintenance engineer's only honest witness to what was actually built. When the drawing and the machine disagree, work stops until somebody finds out which one is lying.

§2 · Revision control — the drawing's chain of custody

Every drawing carries a title block (who, what, when, scale, projection symbol, drawing number) and a revision table (what changed, when, approved by whom). The revision letter is the single most important character on the sheet: torque a coupling per Rev B when the shop built Rev D and the bolts may be the wrong length. The discipline is absolute: work only from the revision the document register calls current, and treat uncontrolled prints — the ones taped inside panel doors — as rumors.

§3 · As-built, as-designed, as-found

Plants drift away from their drawings. Nozzles get relocated, supports added, impellers trimmed. An as-built drawing claims to capture reality after construction; in practice the claim decays from the day it's stamped. The maintenance engineer's habit: verify the two or three dimensions that matter against the metal before cutting steel, and red-line the drawing when reality wins. A marked-up print returned to document control is a gift to the next engineer; a silent discrepancy is a trap armed for them.

Closing. Drawings rule because they are the only durable, arguable record of intent. Respect the revision letter, distrust uncontrolled prints, and feed corrections back — the drawing system is a living asset exactly as much as the machines it describes.
1.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • None yet — this is the course's gate. It defines the vocabulary the next ten lessons use.

Glossary

  • Title block — the sheet's identity card: number, title, scale, projection, approvals.
  • Revision — a controlled change to a released drawing, lettered and logged.
  • Document register — the authoritative list of current revisions.
  • As-built — drawing updated to reflect what was actually constructed.
  • Red-line — field markup recording reality for document control to absorb.
  • Uncontrolled copy — any print outside the register's custody; convenient and dangerous.
1.examples:
Worked example 1 — the revision audit

Problem. A pump baseplate drawing exists as Rev A (issued 2011), Rev C (2016, "anchor bolt pattern revised"), and Rev D (2019, "grout pocket added"). The workshop print in the job pack says Rev C. Find: what must be checked before fabrication proceeds.

Solution. (1) Query the register for the current revision — it is D. (2) Diff C→D via the revision table: the grout pocket affects the pour, not the bolt pattern. (3) Decision: fabrication of the bolt pattern may proceed per C only if D's change is confirmed irrelevant to it; the job pack is nonetheless corrected to Rev D before any work is signed off.

Answer: update the pack to Rev D; verify via the revision description that the C→D delta doesn't touch the work at hand. Sanity check: the failure mode avoided — pouring a base with no grout pocket — costs a jackhammer and a week; the check cost five minutes.

Worked example 2 — which drawing wins?

Problem. During a seal change, the mechanic reports the shaft sleeve measures 44.96 mm; the drawing says 45.00 h6. The stores catalog lists sleeves at both 45.00 and 44.95. Find: the correct action.

Solution. (1) h6 on 45 mm allows 45.000/44.984 — a 44.96 sleeve is out of drawing tolerance, so either wear or a substituted part. (2) Check the machine's history: a previous work order fitted the 44.95 catalog sleeve with a matching seal. (3) The machine's configuration record, not the original drawing, governs the spare to order — and a red-line goes to document control proposing the drawing note "alternate sleeve 44.95 fitted 2019, WO #—."

Answer: order to the as-fitted configuration; red-line the drawing. Sanity check: ordering blind to the drawing would have delivered a sleeve 0.05 mm proud of the fitted seal's working range — a leak on startup.
1.kuwait: Vignette — the panel-door print. You arrive at a packaging line stoppage where the electrician is wiring from a print taped inside the MCC door — hand-annotated, no revision letter, edges soft with age. The controlled drawing, two revisions newer, moved the interlock he is about to bridge. Nothing failed yet; you stop the work anyway, pull the current sheet from the register, and the "fault" resolves in twenty minutes without the bridge. The uncontrolled print goes in the bin with a little ceremony. Standards context: ISO 7200 governs title blocks, but the deeper rule is older than any standard — the register is the truth.

2.lecture:
§1 · Projection — flattening three dimensions honestly

Orthographic projection maps a 3-D object onto 2-D planes with parallel rays, preserving true lengths and angles in each view. The object is imagined inside a glass box; each face of the box receives one view. Unfold the box and the views land in fixed positions relative to each other — that fixed arrangement is what lets a trained reader reconstruct the solid.

§2 · First angle versus third angle

Two unfolding conventions rule the world. In first angle (ISO default, most of Europe and Asia), the object sits between observer and plane: the top view lands below the front view. In third angle (ASME, North America), the plane sits between observer and object: the top view lands above. The truncated-cone symbol in the title block declares which convention the sheet uses — read it first, every sheet, because a first-angle drawing read with third-angle eyes puts every feature on the wrong side of the part.

§3 · Choosing and reading views

The front view carries the most shape information; additional views exist only to remove ambiguity. Hidden lines (dashed) show edges behind material; center lines (chain) declare axes and symmetry. A competent drawing shows the minimum views that fully define the part — and a competent reader cross-references features between views until the solid clicks into place mentally. That click is the skill; it comes only from reps.

Closing. Orthographic views are a lossless code for shape. Learn the two unfolding conventions, check the symbol before reading, and practice the mental refold until drawings become pictures.
2.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Drawing custody (Lesson 1) — you are reading the current revision.
  • Basic solid visualization — rotating simple prisms and cylinders mentally.

Glossary

  • Orthographic projection — parallel-ray mapping preserving true size per view.
  • First/third angle — the two view-arrangement conventions; declared by symbol.
  • Hidden line — dashed; an edge behind material.
  • Center line — chain line marking axes and symmetry.
  • Auxiliary view — projection onto an inclined plane to show a slanted face true-size.
2.examples:
Worked example 1 — which convention?

Problem. A drawing shows a stepped shaft: front view with the larger diameter on the left; the view placed below the front view shows a keyway slot. Find: if the sheet is first-angle, which face of the shaft carries the keyway?

Solution. (1) First angle: the view below the front is the top view (object between observer and plane — projections fall through). (2) A keyway visible in the top view lies on the shaft's upper surface as drawn in the front view. (3) Had the sheet been third angle, the same placement would be the bottom view and the keyway would sit underneath.

Answer: on top (first angle). Sanity check: one symbol, two opposite machining setups — this is why the convention symbol outranks every other mark on the sheet.

Worked example 2 — minimum views

Problem. Decide the minimum view set for (a) a plain cylindrical bushing, (b) an L-shaped bracket with two hole patterns on perpendicular faces.

Solution. (1) Bushing: one view plus a diameter symbol — the ⌀ mark encodes the revolution, so a single longitudinal view fully defines it. (2) Bracket: two views minimum (front + top or front + side) because perpendicular faces each carry features; a third view or an auxiliary is added only if hole positions remain ambiguous.

Answer: (a) one view; (b) two, sometimes three. Sanity check: every extra view is another place for revisions to miss — minimum views is quality practice, not laziness.
2.kuwait: Vignette — mirror-image bracket. You arrive at a fabrication shop holding a freshly made support bracket that bolts up perfectly — mirrored. The drawing came from an overseas licensor in first angle; the shop, used to third-angle prints, read the side view on the wrong side. The truncated-cone symbol was on the sheet all along, small and ignored. The re-make is charged to "training," and the shop adds a one-line stamp to its job traveler: projection convention checked. Cheap lesson at one bracket; expensive at one heat exchanger.

3.lecture:
§1 · Pictorials — communication, not measurement

Isometric and other pictorial views show three faces at once, trading measurability for immediacy. In isometric projection the three axes sit 120° apart and equal lengths along each axis draw equal — but diagonals and circles distort: a hole becomes an ellipse, a square face a rhombus. Pictorials excel at telling a fitter which way parts face; they are the wrong place to scale a dimension, ever.

§2 · Where pictorials mislead

Isometric distortion hides interferences and flattens depth: two edges that clear by millimetres can appear to collide, and coaxial features can appear offset. The professional rule: sight the pictorial for orientation and sequence, then verify geometry in the orthographic views. Exploded isometrics — parts strung along assembly axes — are the fitter's map for teardown order, which is why parts catalogs live on them.

§3 · Freehand sketching — the engineer's fastest tool

A proportioned freehand sketch remains the fastest CAD in existence: boxing in overall proportions first, then carving features, keeping the three isometric axes disciplined. On the floor you will sketch worn parts for the machine shop, mark up failure locations, and capture as-found configurations — with a pencil beating a laptop to every one of these tasks. Proportion matters more than beauty: a sketch with honest ratios and three good dimensions makes a part; a pretty sketch with vague ratios makes scrap.

Closing. Pictorials orient, orthographics measure, and a disciplined freehand sketch is a professional instrument — practice all three deliberately.
3.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Orthographic fluency (Lesson 2) — pictorials complement, never replace, the views.
  • Axis discipline — holding 120° axes steady on paper.

Glossary

  • Isometric — pictorial with equal-scaled axes at 120°.
  • Exploded view — assembly drawn apart along its assembly axes.
  • Foreshortening — apparent shrinkage of lines not parallel to a projection plane.
  • Boxing-in — sketch technique: overall proportions first, features second.
3.examples:
Worked example 1 — the ellipse that is a hole

Problem. In an isometric view, a hole on the top face appears as an ellipse with major axis 20 mm. Find: can the true hole diameter be read from the pictorial, and what is the correct source?

Solution. (1) In isometric projection circles on axis-aligned faces draw as ellipses; scale along the view varies with direction, so the ellipse's axes are not the hole's diameter. (2) The true ⌀ comes only from the dimension callout or the orthographic view. (3) If forced to estimate: the isometric ellipse's major axis ≈ 1.22× the true diameter (projection factor), so ⌀ ≈ 16.4 mm — an estimate for sanity only, never for manufacture.

Answer: read the callout (≈⌀16.4 estimated); never dimension from a pictorial. Sanity check: the 1.22 factor is the isometric distortion \( \sqrt{3/2} \) — geometry, not opinion.

Worked example 2 — sketching a worn bush for remanufacture

Problem. A failed bronze bush must be re-made today; no drawing exists. Find: the minimum sketch content that lets a machine shop produce it.

Solution. (1) Boxed proportioned section sketch: OD, ID, length. (2) Measured dimensions with the instrument stated (vernier ±0.02): OD 60.02, ID 45.05 (worn — spec from shaft: 45.00 H7 running fit, Lesson 6), length 50.0, chamfers 1×45°. (3) Material note (bronze, from spark/color/history), one fit note ("press in housing ⌀60 H7"), date, machine tag, your name.

Answer: a one-page sectioned sketch with five dimensions, material, fit note, and provenance. Sanity check: the shop can machine from it without a phone call — the test every field sketch must pass.
3.kuwait: Vignette — the exploded view that ran the outage. You arrive at a gearbox rebuild where the OEM manual's exploded isometric is the only assembly reference. The crew photographs each stage, but it's the exploded view taped to the bench — parts numbered along the assembly axis — that sequences the rebuild and catches the thrust-washer that "didn't seem to go anywhere." You add one improvement for next time: a freehand sketch of the shim stack with measured thicknesses, because the pictorial shows order, not sizes. Orientation from the picture; numbers from the calipers.

4.lecture:
§1 · Cutting the part open on paper

Interior features drawn as forests of hidden lines become unreadable fast. A section view slices the part with an imaginary cutting plane and draws what the knife exposes: solid material carries hatching; voids stay clear. The cutting-plane line in the parent view — with arrows showing viewing direction and letters naming the section — is the key that connects slice to view.

§2 · The section family

Full sections cut straight across. Half sections show half-in, half-out on symmetric parts. Offset sections bend the cutting plane to pass through features that don't share a line — the bends are never drawn in the section itself. Revolved and removed sections drop a cross-section onto or beside a long member; broken-out sections peel a small window. Choosing the right family is drawing craft: the goal is always the fewest, clearest slices.

§3 · Conventions that lie honestly

Ribs, webs, shafts, bolts, and keys are conventionally not hatched even when the plane slices them — hatching them would suggest massive solidity and hide the part's real structure. Similarly, spokes rotate into the plane, and lugs align by convention. These are deliberate, standardized departures from literal truth that make drawings more truthful about function. Knowing them separates readers from decoders.

Closing. Sections replace hidden-line archaeology with visible truth; the conventions — unhatched ribs and fasteners above all — are shared grammar, not license.
4.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Orthographic views (Lesson 2) — sections are views with a knife.
  • Hidden-line reading — to appreciate what sectioning removes.

Glossary

  • Cutting plane — the imaginary knife; its line, arrows, and letters index the section.
  • Hatching — thin parallel lines marking cut material; pattern hints at material family.
  • Full/half/offset section — the main slice strategies.
  • Broken-out section — a local peel, bounded by a freehand break line.
  • Convention — a standardized deliberate simplification (unhatched ribs, bolts, shafts).
4.examples:
Worked example 1 — choosing the slice

Problem. A pump casing has: a suction bore on the centerline, a gauge boss 40 mm off-center, and a drain boss lower still — none collinear. Find: the section strategy that shows all three bores in one view.

Solution. (1) A straight full section catches only the suction bore. (2) An offset section routes the cutting plane through all three features with two right-angle jogs. (3) The parent view shows the jogged cutting-plane line A–A; the section view draws the three bores as if they were coplanar, jogs invisible.

Answer: offset section A–A. Sanity check: three separate sections would say less with three times the ink — the offset section exists precisely for this case.

Worked example 2 — why isn't the rib hatched?

Problem. A sectioned bracket shows its central stiffening rib unhatched while the flanges carry hatching; a trainee flags it as a drafting error. Find: the ruling and the reason.

Solution. (1) Standard convention (ASME Y14.3 / ISO 128 practice): ribs and webs cut longitudinally are shown unhatched, to avoid the false impression of a solid block. (2) The convention preserves the reader's sense of the part as thin structure with a stiffener, which is the mechanical truth that matters for strength and casting alike.

Answer: not an error — convention. Sanity check: hatch the rib mentally and the bracket reads as a solid wedge — heavier, stiffer, and wrong.
4.kuwait: Vignette — the void that wasn't on the print. You arrive at a valve refurbishment where the machinist, boring out a seat, breaks into an unexpected cored passage. The drawing's single full section missed it; the casting's offset drain gallery ran exactly where nobody sliced. The repair becomes a weld build-up job, and your close-out note to document control requests one addition: an offset section through the gallery on the next revision. Sections are cheap; discovering geometry with a boring bar is not.

5.lecture:
§1 · Dimensions carry function

A dimension is not decoration; it encodes which distances matter and from where. Good practice dimensions each feature once, from datums that mean something to the part's function, placed in the view where the feature shows true shape. Duplicated or over-complete dimensioning is an error, not generosity: two paths to the same edge will one day disagree, and the shop must guess which one you meant.

§2 · Chains, baselines, and the accumulation problem

Chain dimensioning strings features end-to-end: each step is controlled, but the position of the last feature accumulates every tolerance in the chain — three steps at ±0.1 can put the final hole ±0.3 from the first. Baseline dimensioning measures every feature from one datum: each position is held directly, and accumulation dies. The choice is functional: chain when step-to-step spacing matters (mating hole pairs), baseline when positions from a reference matter (mounting patterns).

§3 · The grammar of callouts

⌀ before a value means diameter; R radius; ( ) marks a reference dimension (information, not control); a boxed dimension is theoretically exact — controlled elsewhere, usually by GD&T. Units are declared once in the title block; mixed units on a face of a drawing are a defect. Reading a dimension means reading its status: controlled, reference, or basic — three different levels of promise.

Closing. Dimension once, from functional datums, chain or baseline by intent, and read every callout's status before trusting it. The dimensions are the part's legal description; everything else is scenery.
5.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Views and sections (Lessons 2, 4) — dimensions live where shape shows true.
  • Tolerance intuition — every dimension implies a permitted variation (Lesson 6 formalizes).

Glossary

  • Datum — the reference a dimension originates from.
  • Chain dimensioning — feature-to-feature; accumulates tolerance.
  • Baseline dimensioning — all from one datum; kills accumulation.
  • Reference dimension — parenthesized; informative, not binding.
  • Basic dimension — boxed; exact by definition, toleranced through GD&T.
5.examples:
Worked example 1 — accumulation, quantified

Problem. Four holes in a strip are chain-dimensioned 50±0.1, 50±0.1, 50±0.1 from hole 1. Find: the worst-case position error of hole 4 relative to hole 1, and the baseline alternative.

Solution. (1) Chain: errors add — worst case \( \pm(0.1+0.1+0.1) = \pm0.3 \) mm. (2) Baseline: dimension holes at 50±0.1, 100±0.1, 150±0.1 from hole 1 — each position now held to ±0.1 directly. (3) Note the trade: baseline loosens control of spacing between adjacent holes (two ±0.1 positions → spacing ±0.2), so the functional question — do positions or spacings matter? — decides the scheme.

Answer: chain worst case ±0.3; baseline holds each position to ±0.1 at the cost of ±0.2 on adjacent spacing. Sanity check: tolerances add linearly in worst case — three dice, three chances to land long.

Worked example 2 — the double-dimension trap

Problem. A plate 120 mm long carries a slot dimensioned 40 from the left edge and 80 from the right edge, both toleranced ±0.2. Find: the defect.

Solution. (1) The dimensions over-define the slot: 40 + 80 = 120 only if the plate is exactly 120. (2) With the plate itself at 120±0.3, a shop holding both slot dimensions is being asked to control the plate length through the slot — impossible as written. (3) Fix: one dimension governs (say 40±0.2 from the functional edge); the other becomes reference (80) or is deleted.

Answer: over-dimensioning; demote one dimension to reference. Sanity check: ask "could both be simultaneously out?" If yes, the scheme fights itself.
5.kuwait: Vignette — the base that wouldn't bolt down. You arrive at a motor swap where the new base's holes miss the foundation bolts by 2 mm on the far pair — every hole individually "in tolerance" on the shop's report. The drawing chained the pattern; the tolerances accumulated exactly as Lesson-5 arithmetic predicts, and the far holes wandered. The concrete wins arguments with steel, so the base gets slotted this time — and the drawing gets a baseline-dimensioned revision so the next base arrives right. You attach worked example 1 to the revision request verbatim; document control approves it same-day.

6.lecture:
§1 · Tolerance is a purchase

No process makes exact dimensions; tolerance is how much inexactness the function can afford. Tightening a tolerance buys precision with money — grinding instead of turning, gauging instead of measuring. The engineer's discipline is to tolerance functionally: as loose as the function allows, as tight as it demands, feature by feature. Blanket tight tolerances are a tax the shop invoices back.

§2 · The ISO fit system

Cylindrical fits are standardized as a letter-number pair on each member: the letter locates the tolerance zone (A…H clearance side for holes, h…z for shafts), the number (IT grade) sets its width. The hole-basis convention keeps the hole at H (zone starting at nominal) and moves the shaft:

\[ \text{H7/g6: running clearance} \qquad \text{H7/k6: transition} \qquad \text{H7/p6: interference} \tag{1} \]

For ⌀45 H7/g6: hole 45.000/45.025, shaft 44.991/44.975 — clearance between 0.009 and 0.050 mm: a shaft that turns freely with minimal slop, the classic bearing-journal recipe.

§3 · Reading limits like a fitter

Every fit resolves to four numbers — hole max/min, shaft max/min — and two extremes: tightest (min hole, max shaft) and loosest (max hole, min shaft). Compute both before assembly decisions: will it press, slide, or rattle? Bearing catalogs, coupling manuals, and seal datasheets all speak this language; the fitter's micrometer settles the argument.

Closing. Tolerances price function; the ISO letter-number system encodes fits compactly; and four limit numbers per joint tell you tight, loose, and everything a rebuild needs to know.
6.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Dimensioning (Lesson 5) — limits attach to dimensions.
  • Decimal discipline — thousandths of a millimetre matter here.

Glossary

  • IT grade — tolerance-zone width class (IT6 tighter than IT8).
  • Hole basis — hole held at H; shaft letter sets the fit.
  • Clearance/transition/interference — always loose / could be either / always tight.
  • Allowance — the tightest intended condition of a fit.
  • Limits — the max and min values a dimension may take.
6.examples:
Worked example 1 — decode ⌀45 H7/g6 completely

Problem. For ⌀45 H7/g6 (hole 45.000/45.025; shaft 44.991/44.975), find the extreme clearances and classify the fit.

Solution. (1) Tightest: min hole − max shaft = 45.000 − 44.991 = 0.009 mm. (2) Loosest: max hole − min shaft = 45.025 − 44.975 = 0.050 mm. (3) Both positive → pure clearance fit; magnitudes (9–50 µm on ⌀45) → precision running fit.

Answer: clearance 0.009–0.050 mm; precision running fit (journals, sliding hubs). Sanity check: clearance ≈ 0.2–1‰ of diameter — the classic oil-film neighborhood.

Worked example 2 — will it press or slide?

Problem. A coupling hub is bored ⌀60 H7 (60.000/60.030); the shaft end measures 60.021 on site. Find: the assembly condition across the hole's range.

Solution. (1) Against min hole: 60.000 − 60.021 = −0.021 → 21 µm interference. (2) Against max hole: 60.030 − 60.021 = +0.009 → 9 µm clearance. (3) The joint is transition: this specific hub may press on or slip on depending on its actual bore — measure the hub, then decide heating or pressing.

Answer: between 21 µm tight and 9 µm loose — measure the mating bore before method selection. Sanity check: transition fits exist exactly for keyed couplings: snug location, feasible assembly; the k6 shaft zone straddling nominal is the giveaway.
6.kuwait: Vignette — the bearing that spun its seat. You arrive at a repeated bearing failure: inner races spinning on a fan shaft, blued and scored. Site measurements tell the story the work orders didn't — the shaft journal, polished by three "emergency" rebuilds, sits 30 µm under its k5 zone: what should be a light transition fit became running clearance under load. The metallurgy is fine; the fit died. Repair by metal-spray and regrind back to limits, and the failure mode disappears. The lesson you brief the crew: a bearing seat is a toleranced dimension with a job, and every polish "just to clean it up" spends the fit.

7.lecture:
§1 · Why coordinates aren't enough

Plus-minus tolerances on coordinates control a hole's X and Y separately — but function cares about the hole's position as a whole, its axis attitude, the flatness of the face it lives on. Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing (GD&T) tolerances geometry directly: form (flatness, straightness), orientation (perpendicularity, parallelism), location (position, concentricity-family), and runout. Each control is a feature-control frame: symbol, tolerance zone, and the datums it's measured from.

§2 · Datums — the measurement contract

A datum reference frame (A|B|C) declares the exact sequence of surfaces the part sits on during measurement: primary A arrests three degrees of freedom, secondary B two, tertiary C one. The same part, gauged from different datums, yields different verdicts — so the drawing fixes the frame once, and design, manufacture, and inspection all argue inside it. Datums are chosen from function: the faces that seat, the bores that locate.

§3 · Runout — the rotating machine's own control

Circular runout limits the wobble of each circular element as the part turns on its datum axis — one dial reading per section. Total runout sweeps the whole surface axially and radially at once, controlling taper and coning that circular runout cannot see. For couplings, sleeves, and seal faces, runout tolerances are the drawing's way of promising your dial indicator a quiet needle — and Lesson 6's fits plus today's runout are the complete language of a shaft drawing.

Closing. GD&T tolerances what function feels — form, orientation, location, runout — from declared datums. Read the frame, find the datums, and the drawing tells you exactly how the inspector will set the part up.
7.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Limits and fits (Lesson 6) — GD&T rides on toleranced features.
  • Datum thinking (Lesson 5) — formalized here into A|B|C frames.

Glossary

  • Feature-control frame — the boxed callout: symbol | zone | datums.
  • Datum reference frame — the seating sequence that arrests the part for measurement.
  • Position tolerance — a zone (usually ⌀) the feature's axis must live in.
  • MMC — maximum material condition; enables bonus tolerance and functional gauging.
  • Circular vs total runout — per-section wobble vs whole-surface sweep.
7.examples:
Worked example 1 — position beats coordinates

Problem. A hole is located X = 50±0.1, Y = 50±0.1. Find: the worst-case radial position error, and the equivalent GD&T callout that controls it directly.

Solution. (1) Worst case: both coordinates off by 0.1 → radial error \( \sqrt{0.1^2 + 0.1^2} = 0.141 \) mm; the coordinate scheme actually permits a square zone whose corners stick out 41% farther than its sides. (2) GD&T equivalent: basic dimensions 50, 50 with position ⌀0.28 (A|B|C) — a round zone of the same worst-case radius, uniform in all directions. (3) Bonus: add Ⓜ and the zone grows as the hole departs MMC — functional gauging becomes possible.

Answer: ±0.1 coordinates permit 0.141 radial error; position ⌀0.28 controls the same function honestly. Sanity check: the diagonal of a square vs its side — the 41% is pure Pythagoras.

Worked example 2 — circular passes, total fails

Problem. A seal sleeve shows circular runout 0.02 at every measured section, yet total runout measures 0.09. Find: what geometry explains this, and which control the seal cares about.

Solution. (1) Each section is individually round about the axis (0.02) but the sections don't line up: the surface is tapered or coned — radius drifts along the length. (2) Total runout sweeps axially and accumulates that drift (0.09). (3) A lip seal riding that sleeve sees a changing interference along its width — total runout is the functional control for seal tracks and coupling rims.

Answer: taper/coning; specify total runout for seal surfaces. Sanity check: circular runout at many sections plus a straightness check ≈ total runout — the 0.07 gap is the taper the single-section dial could never see.
7.kuwait: Vignette — the coupling that "passed" and still shook. You arrive at a pump train that vibrates at 1× despite laser alignment certificates. The coupling hub's rim runout was checked — circular, mid-face, 0.02, pass. Swept across the full rim face, total runout comes in at 0.11: the hub face is coned from a previous overheat, and the alignment laser has been faithfully aligning to a lie. The hub is refaced; the 1× drops by two-thirds. Your close-out adds one line to the site's alignment procedure: face runout means total, swept, per the drawing's GD&T — not one ring at one radius.

8.lecture:
§1 · Symbols — high-density engineering language

Drawings compress whole procedures into symbols. Three families dominate plant work: threads and fasteners, welds, and surface finish. Misread any of them and the error is built, not drawn. This lesson is deliberately a reading lesson: the symbols carry law.

§2 · Threads and welds

M16×2 6g reads: ISO metric thread, 16 mm nominal, 2 mm pitch, external tolerance class 6g. Coarse pitch is default; fine pitch is specified. For welds, the ISO/AWS symbol hangs instructions on a reference line: the symbol below the line applies to the arrow side of the joint, above the line to the other side; numbers left of the symbol give throat or leg size, right of it length and pitch; a flag means field weld; a circle means weld-all-around. A fillet symbol with 6 below the line and 6 above commands both sides at 6 mm — miss the top symbol and half the strength stays undelivered.

§3 · Surface finish

The checkmark symbol carries roughness (Ra in µm), sometimes process notes and lay direction. Ra 3.2 is honest machining; 1.6 careful turning; 0.8 grinding territory; 0.4 and below, lapping and money. Finish symbols on seal journals, gasket faces, and bearing seats are functional specifications: a seal lip on an Ra 3.2 journal wears out years early, a gasket on a too-smooth flange face slides instead of gripping. The number is a friction, sealing, and fatigue decision — never cosmetic.

Closing. Thread callouts, weld symbols, and finish marks are compressed contracts. Read them slowly once, and the shop reads your intent forever.
8.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Sections and dimensions (Lessons 4–5) — symbols attach to defined geometry.
  • Fits (Lesson 6) — thread classes are the fit system's cousins.

Glossary

  • Pitch — axial advance per thread turn (mm).
  • Arrow side / other side — the weld symbol's two shores of the reference line.
  • Throat / leg — a fillet weld's two size languages (a ≈ 0.7z).
  • Field weld flag — welded at site, not in the shop.
  • Ra — arithmetic average roughness, µm.
8.examples:
Worked example 1 — decode a weld callout

Problem. A gusset drawing shows a fillet symbol below the line marked "z6", the same symbol above the line, a circle at the bend, and a flag. Find: the complete instruction.

Solution. (1) Fillet below line: 6 mm leg on the arrow side. (2) Fillet above: same weld on the other side — both sides welded. (3) Circle: all around the joint's perimeter. (4) Flag: executed at site, not in the shop.

Answer: 6 mm double-sided all-around fillet, field welded. Sanity check: leg 6 → throat ≈ 4.2 mm; if the design note requires a 4 mm throat, the callout closes it with margin.

Worked example 2 — the finish that seals

Problem. A shaft drawing marks the seal journal Ra 0.4 with lay circumferential, while the adjacent bearing seat reads Ra 0.8. The repair shop proposes "polishing everything to a mirror." Find: the ruling.

Solution. (1) Ra 0.4 circumferential on the seal track: the lip needs a smooth surface with micro-texture oriented so it pumps lubricant under the lip — mirror-smooth (Ra ≈ 0.1) can actually starve and cook the lip. (2) The bearing seat's 0.8 supports the fit's friction; over-polishing lowers grip of the interference. (3) Ruling: finish to the numbers, not beyond them.

Answer: hold Ra 0.4 (circumferential lay) and Ra 0.8 respectively; "better" finish is worse function. Sanity check: seal manufacturers specify Ra bands (typ. 0.2–0.8) with plunge-ground lay — bands, not ceilings, because both edges bite.
8.kuwait: Vignette — the missing top fillet. You arrive at a cracked conveyor-support gusset, eighteen months old. The drawing commanded double-sided fillets — symbol above and below the line; the fabricator welded the visible side only, and paint hid the omission until fatigue found it. The repair is one evening's welding; the systemic fix is a fabrication-QC checklist line — weld symbols verified both sides of reference line — and a toolbox talk where you draw the symbol grammar on the whiteboard. AWS A2.4 in ten minutes, cheaper than the next crack.

9.lecture:
§1 · Parametric means rebuildable

Parametric CAD stores recipes, not shapes: a sketch with dimensions, then features — extrude, revolve, hole, fillet — stacked in a history tree. Change a dimension and the recipe re-executes. The power is enormous and conditional: a model rebuilt from a disciplined recipe updates cleanly; a model built on accidental geometry collapses into red errors the first time anyone touches it.

§2 · Design intent — the recipe's grammar

Design intent is deciding what should stay true when things change. A hole 10 mm from the edge (offset intent) behaves differently from a hole at the plate's midline (symmetry intent) when the plate grows. The discipline: fully define every sketch (no under-constrained geometry drifting at a click), reference datums and axes rather than face edges that may vanish, name features, and order the tree so machining-like operations come last. Model as if the next engineer will change the one dimension you least expect — because they will.

§3 · From model to manufacture

The model is master; drawings, CAM paths, and 3-D prints are projections of it. Materials, mass properties, and revision metadata live on the model. For the maintenance engineer, the skill is bidirectional: read OEM models when you have them, and rebuild worn parts as clean parametric models when you don't — Lesson 3's field sketch becomes tonight's model and tomorrow's machined spare.

Closing. Parametric CAD is programming with geometry: fully defined sketches, functional references, an ordered tree — that is what separates a model from a liability.
9.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Views and dimensioning (Lessons 2, 5) — sketch constraints are dimensions with intent.
  • Datum thinking (Lesson 7) — model references mirror measurement datums.

Glossary

  • Feature tree — the ordered recipe of operations.
  • Fully defined sketch — zero degrees of freedom; nothing drifts.
  • Design intent — the declared invariants under change.
  • Reference geometry — planes/axes/points created to anchor features stably.
  • Rebuild error — the tree failing after an upstream change; the tax on lazy references.
9.examples:
Worked example 1 — intent, tested

Problem. A 100×60 plate has a ⌀9 hole. Model A dimensions the hole 20 from the left edge; Model B constrains it to the plate's vertical midline. The plate is later widened to 140. Find: where the hole lands in each model, and which intent suits (a) a clearance hole for a fixed stud, (b) a lifting hole.

Solution. (1) Model A: hole stays 20 from the left edge — absolute offset preserved. (2) Model B: hole rides to the new midline (70) — symmetry preserved. (3) Function decides: (a) the stud doesn't move when the plate grows → offset intent (A); (b) lifting wants balance → symmetry intent (B).

Answer: A: x=20; B: x=70; choose by function — (a) A, (b) B. Sanity check: neither model is "right" in the abstract; intent is a functional claim, which is why it must be chosen, not defaulted.

Worked example 2 — the fillet that killed the tree

Problem. A bracket model references a hole sketch to the edge created by a fillet. A revision deletes the fillet; forty downstream features error out. Find: the modeling fault and the repair strategy.

Solution. (1) Fault: sketch referenced consumable geometry (a fillet edge) instead of stable references (datum plane / base edge). (2) Repair: redefine the sketch onto the base face and datum axis, then re-run the tree; add reference planes for any future features. (3) Prevention: reference datums first, faces second, feature edges never — the same hierarchy inspection uses.

Answer: re-anchor to stable references; adopt datum-first modeling. Sanity check: the fix touches one sketch, not forty features — proof the fault was referential, not geometric.
9.kuwait: Vignette — the impeller wear ring, digitized. You arrive at a stores review where a pump's wear rings — obsolete, OEM defunct — are consuming six-week emergency machining cycles from a faded fax of a sketch. One evening of parametric modeling from a measured worn ring (Lesson 3's sketch, Lesson 6's fits restoring the design clearance) produces a clean model with the running clearance driven by one named parameter. The next order is a two-day local machining job with the drawing auto-generated from the model. The fax retires; the parameter ring_clearance is now plant knowledge instead of folklore.

10.lecture:
§1 · Assemblies — constraints as kinematics

An assembly model positions parts with mates: coincident faces, concentric axes, distance offsets. Every mate removes degrees of freedom; the art is removing exactly the right ones. A shaft concentric to a bore and seated on a shoulder should still spin — leave that freedom alive and the model tells you how the machine moves; over-constrain it and the model fights itself with warnings that mean nothing and hide the one that does.

§2 · The BOM is the assembly's ledger

The bill of materials — item numbers ballooned on the drawing, quantities, part numbers, materials — is what stores, procurement, and the fitter actually consume. Balloon 7 on the exploded view is line 7 in the table is the box on the shelf. Structure matters: sub-assemblies roll up (a bearing unit is one line here, four lines in its own BOM), and phantom items (bulk grease, thread-locker) appear where policy puts them. A drawing whose balloons and BOM disagree is a stores error factory.

§3 · Drawings from models — still a craft

CAD auto-generates views, but choosing them is still Lesson 2's judgment: which section shows the fits, where the BOM table sits, which dimensions the fitter needs at the machine (coupling gap, seal compression, bolt torque table). Auto-ballooning follows assembly structure; verify it — a regenerated drawing can renumber balloons silently between revisions, and a fitter working from memory of "item 3" will draw the wrong part.

Closing. Mates encode kinematics, BOMs encode logistics, and the assembly drawing binds them for the person with the spanner. Model the motion, audit the balloons.
10.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Part modeling (Lesson 9) — assemblies consume disciplined parts.
  • Exploded views (Lesson 3) — the assembly drawing's teaching view.

Glossary

  • Mate/constraint — a positional relation removing degrees of freedom.
  • Degrees of freedom — the six motions a free rigid body owns.
  • BOM — the ballooned parts ledger of an assembly.
  • Sub-assembly — a rolled-up unit with its own BOM.
  • Interference check — the assembly's clash audit before metal exists.
10.examples:
Worked example 1 — count the freedoms

Problem. A pump shaft is mated into an assembly: concentric to the bearing bore, face-coincident at the shaft shoulder. Find: the remaining degrees of freedom, and state whether the assembly is correctly constrained for a rotating machine model.

Solution. (1) Free body: 6 DOF. (2) Concentric (axis-to-axis): removes 4 — two translations and two rotations transverse to the axis. (3) Face coincidence at the shoulder: removes axial translation (1 more). (4) Remaining: rotation about the axis — exactly the freedom a shaft must keep.

Answer: 1 DOF (spin) — correctly constrained. Sanity check: add a keyway parallel mate and DOF drops to 0: right for a static check, wrong for motion studies — constraint choice follows the question being asked.

Worked example 2 — the balloon audit

Problem. Rev F of a gearbox drawing ballooned the input seal as item 9; stores stocked shelf 9 accordingly. Rev G inserted a spacer as item 5, shifting the seal to item 10, and the drawing was reissued without a change note on numbering. Find: the failure mode and the two-line fix.

Solution. (1) Failure: fitters ordering "item 9" now receive the spacer's neighbor — wrong part at 2 a.m., the classic renumbering trap. (2) Fix line 1: BOMs reference immutable part numbers, balloons are per-revision pointers only — train and enforce. (3) Fix line 2: revision note must state "items renumbered" whenever structure changes, and stores cross-references by part number, never balloon.

Answer: renumbered balloons + part-number-blind ordering; enforce part-number ordering and renumbering notes. Sanity check: the failure needs no bad actor — two correct documents, one missing sentence.
10.kuwait: Vignette — the 2 a.m. wrong seal. You arrive the morning after a night crew rebuilt a filler gearbox with "item 9 per the wall drawing" — an uncontrolled Rev F print (Lesson 1's ghost returns) whose numbering Rev G had shifted. The machine ran eight hours before the too-narrow seal let go. Root cause is boring and structural: balloon-ordering culture plus an uncontrolled print. The fix package: register-only prints in the shop, part-number ordering policy, and a BOM audit of the five most-rebuilt assets. Nobody gets named; the system does.

11.lecture:
§1 · The plant's drawing ecosystem

Beyond part and assembly drawings, a plant runs on: GA (general arrangement) drawings — equipment in space, with footprints, elevations, and maintenance clearances; P&IDs — process logic, every pipe, valve, and instrument as symbols (INS 352 goes deep); isometrics — pipe runs dimensioned for fabrication; foundation and structural drawings — what the equipment stands on; and electrical single-lines — power distribution's family tree. Each answers one question well; the reader's skill is knowing which sheet answers today's question.

§2 · Navigating a GA

A GA is read in three passes. Pass one: orientation — north arrow, column grid, elevation datums. Pass two: the equipment item and its immediate space — footprint, anchor pattern, withdrawal clearances (tube bundle pull, rotor removal envelope, crane hook travel). Pass three: the cross-references — detail drawings, foundation sheets, vendor drawings called up by number. The grid reference (column line C-7) is the plant's postal address system; learn to speak it and rigging studies stop being arguments.

§3 · The drawing package as a system

A pump replacement touches the GA (space and clearances), the foundation drawing (bolts and grout), the vendor GA (nozzle loads, base details), the P&ID (what it connects to), an isometric (how the pipe reaches it), and the single-line (its feeder). The engineer's product is often not a drawing but a package: the current revisions of all of these, cross-checked. Lesson 1's register discipline scales up here — a package with one stale sheet is a stale package.

Closing. The course ends where it began: drawings as the plant's contracts. You can now read the whole family — parts, assemblies, GAs — with revision discipline, datum eyes, and symbol fluency. DRW 102's exam is every job pack you'll ever assemble.
11.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • The whole course — Lessons 1–10 supply custody, views, sections, dimensions, fits, GD&T, symbols, and CAD; this lesson deploys them at plant scale.

Glossary

  • GA drawing — general arrangement: equipment in plant space.
  • P&ID — piping & instrumentation diagram; process logic, not geometry.
  • Isometric (piping) — fabrication drawing of a pipe run.
  • Column grid — the lettered/numbered address system of a plant.
  • Withdrawal clearance — reserved space to pull bundles, rotors, filters.
  • Drawing package — the cross-checked set of current sheets for one job.
11.examples:
Worked example 1 — will the new pump fit?

Problem. A replacement pump is 180 mm longer than the original. The GA shows the pump at grid E-4, discharge toward north, 1,100 mm clear to a cable tray column; the vendor GA gives the new overall length and the same anchor pattern. Find: the fit verdict and the checks that produce it.

Solution. (1) Anchor pattern identical → foundation unchanged (verify against the foundation sheet's bolt layout anyway — Lesson 5's accumulation ghosts live in old foundations). (2) Length grows 180 mm toward the coupling end; GA scaling of the 1,100 mm gap minus 180 leaves 920 mm — above the 800 mm maintenance aisle minimum noted on the GA. (3) Check withdrawal: rotor pulls toward the aisle, envelope still clear.

Answer: fits, with 920 mm aisle retained; foundation reused. Sanity check: every number came off a controlled sheet, not a tape measure — and the site verification tape measure comes anyway, because as-builts drift (Lesson 1).

Worked example 2 — assembling the job package

Problem. Scope: replace the E-4 pump. Find: the minimum drawing package and the currency check.

Solution. (1) Sheets: plant GA (E-4 area), foundation drawing, vendor GA + sectional drawing, P&ID for the loop, suction/discharge isometrics, electrical single-line for the feeder, plus the coupling and seal detail drawings. (2) Currency: pull revisions from the register the same day, list them on the package cover, and mark the package itself with a compile date. (3) One stale sheet — say an isometric missing a rerouted drain — invalidates the pressure-test plan; the register check is the package's foundation.

Answer: eight sheets, register-verified, cover-listed. Sanity check: the package answers space, support, process, pipe, and power — the five questions every equipment change asks.
11.kuwait: Vignette — the bundle with nowhere to go. You arrive at an exchanger cleaning job where the crane is rigged, the bundle is unbolted — and the pull path dead-ends into a pipe rack erected two projects ago. The GA's withdrawal envelope was never updated; the rack's drawing package didn't cross-check the reserved clearance. The bundle waits a week for a scaffold-and-skate solution. Your close-out attaches both drawings side by side and lands one standing rule for the site: no structure inside a marked withdrawal envelope without a GA revision — the drawing system defending physical space the way it defends dimensions.
